meaning: portmgrd successfully applied an MTU value to a kernel network interface via ip link set mtu
likely_causes: Operator applied 'interface mtu <value>' in CONFIG_DB
Automated provisioning pushed a new MTU via RESTAPI or sonic-cfggen
next_steps: Confirm the MTU is active on the kernel interface: ip link show <interface> | grep mtu
Verify CONFIG_DB entry: redis-cli -n 4 HGET 'PORT|<interface>' mtu
Check APPL_DB was updated: redis-cli -n 0 HGET 'PORT_TABLE:<interface>' mtu
If traffic issues persist after MTU change, check end-to-end path MTU: ping -s <size> -M do <peer>

meaning: portmgrd applied an admin-up or admin-down to the kernel interface in response to a CONFIG_DB change
likely_causes: Operator ran 'interface shutdown' or 'no shutdown' via CLI or SONiC config
Automated rollout toggling ports during provisioning
Warm restart replay of admin state from CONFIG_DB
next_steps: Verify the kernel state matches intent: ip link show <interface>
Check CONFIG_DB admin_status: redis-cli -n 4 HGET 'PORT|<interface>' admin_status
Confirm orchagent propagated the change to ASIC: redis-cli -n 0 HGET 'PORT_TABLE:<interface>' admin_status
If port should be up but kernel shows DOWN, check physical link: show interfaces status <interface>
Review recent config changes: show logging | grep portmgrd | tail -20

pattern: Failed to set.*MTU|Failed to set port MTU|error.*mtu.*EOPNOTSUPP|EINVAL.*mtu
meaning: portmgrd attempted to apply an MTU value to a kernel interface but the kernel or driver rejected it
likely_causes: Requested MTU exceeds the driver maximum for this NIC/ASIC (common above 9216 bytes)
Interface is in a bonded/LAG group whose master has a lower MTU
Kernel module does not support jumbo frames
Race condition: interface does not exist in kernel yet when portmgrd tried to configure it
next_steps: Check kernel error details: journalctl -u portmgrd | grep -i mtu | tail -30
Verify driver MTU limits: ip link show <interface> | grep mtu; ethtool -i <interface>
Check if interface is in a portchannel: show interfaces portchannel
If LAG member, set MTU on the portchannel itself, not the member
Confirm platform MTU support in platform.json or hwsku.json
Fallback: set MTU manually to confirm kernel accepts it: ip link set <intf> mtu <value>; if rejected, the value exceeds hardware capability

meaning: portmgrd wrote a new port speed (or autoneg setting) to the kernel interface or notified orchagent via APPL_DB
likely_causes: Operator set interface speed via CLI or config_db.json
Autoneg mode changed alongside speed hint
next_steps: Verify speed is visible: show interfaces status <interface>
Check APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 HGET 'PORT_TABLE:<interface>' speed
Check ASIC_DB to confirm SAI programmed it: redis-cli -n 1 KEYS 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_PORT:*' | xargs -I{} redis-cli -n 1 HGET {} SAI_PORT_ATTR_SPEED
If speed mismatch persists, verify autoneg setting: show interfaces autoneg status <interface>
Check transceiver supports requested speed: show interfaces transceiver eeprom <interface>

meaning: portmgrd applied a Forward Error Correction mode change to the port, writing it to APPL_DB for orchagent/syncd to program into the ASIC
likely_causes: Operator set 'interface fec <mode>' (none, rs, fc) via CLI
Autoneg negotiated a new FEC mode with the peer
next_steps: Verify current FEC mode: show interfaces fec status <interface>
Check APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 HGET 'PORT_TABLE:<interface>' fec
Confirm orchagent programmed the SAI attribute: docker exec swss grep -i fec /var/log/swss/orchagent.log | tail -20
If BER increases after FEC change, check cable quality: show interfaces transceiver eeprom <interface>
RS-FEC required for 100G+ ports on most platforms -- do not disable without careful testing

meaning: portmgrd issued a netlink request to configure the port but received an error response from the kernel
likely_causes: Interface does not exist in the kernel at the time of the netlink call (timing/ordering issue)
Kernel returned ENODEV -- interface was deleted or renamed
Insufficient privileges (should not happen inside swss container but can after custom hardening)
Conflicting configuration from another daemon or script running outside SONiC orchestration
next_steps: Check if interface exists in kernel: ip link show <interface>
Review portmgrd logs for the preceding context: docker exec swss grep portmgrd /var/log/syslog | tail -50
Restart portmgrd if interface is present but state is stale: docker exec swss supervisorctl restart portmgrd
If interface is missing entirely, check if swss orchagent created it: redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL 'PORT_TABLE:<interface>'
Verify no external scripts are managing the same interface: ps aux | grep 'ip link'
As last resort, restart swss container: sudo systemctl restart swss (causes brief traffic disruption)

pattern: waiting for port ready|port.*not ready|port state check.*wait
meaning: portmgrd is polling STATE_DB waiting for orchagent to mark the port as ready before applying further configuration -- a normal but time-bounded wait state
likely_causes: orchagent has not yet created the SAI port object and written 'ok' to STATE_DB:PORT_TABLE
syncd is slow to respond during bootup or warm restart, delaying orchagent
portmgrd started before orchagent finished initializing ports (race at container start)
Port was removed and re-added (e.g., breakout reconfiguration) and portmgrd picked up the CONFIG_DB entry before orchagent reprovisioned it
next_steps: Check if the port is ready in STATE_DB: redis-cli -n 6 HGET 'PORT_TABLE|<interface>' state
Check orchagent is running: docker exec swss supervisorctl status orchagent
Check if orchagent is stuck: docker exec swss grep -i 'error\|fatal' /var/log/swss/orchagent.log | tail -20
Monitor STATE_DB for port readiness: watch -n 1 'redis-cli -n 6 KEYS PORT_TABLE*'
If wait exceeds 60 seconds, check syncd: docker exec syncd supervisorctl status syncd
If after breakout reconfiguration, allow extra time -- portmgrd retries on a timer and will proceed once orchagent completes

meaning: portmgrd applied an interface description or alias to the kernel interface (written to /sys/class/net/<intf>/ifalias or via netlink)
likely_causes: Operator set 'interface description <text>' in CONFIG_DB
Bulk provisioning script updated interface descriptions via sonic-cfggen or config reload
next_steps: Verify alias was applied to kernel: cat /sys/class/net/<interface>/ifalias
Check CONFIG_DB description field: redis-cli -n 4 HGET 'PORT|<interface>' description
Confirm APPL_DB reflects description: redis-cli -n 0 HGET 'PORT_TABLE:<interface>' description
If description is blank but CONFIG_DB has a value, restart portmgrd: docker exec swss supervisorctl restart portmgrd

meaning: syncd detected express boot mode and is initializing SAI with express warm start (type 4). NPU will NOT be programmed until APPLY_VIEW is received.
likely_causes: Normal express reboot boot-up sequence
next_steps: Monitor for APPLY_VIEW notification: docker logs syncd 2>&1 | grep APPLY_VIEW
This is expected -- no action needed unless NPU init never happens

meaning: syncd failed to set the FAST_API_ENABLE attribute on one or more ASICs during express reboot pre-shutdown. The express reboot will abort.
likely_causes: Vendor SAI does not support express boot (non-Cisco-8000 platform)
SDK internal error during the attribute set call
Multi-ASIC system where one ASIC's SAI rejected the call
next_steps: Check platform: show platform summary (must be Cisco 8000)
Check full syncd log: docker logs syncd 2>&1 | tail -50
If platform is correct, this may be an SDK bug -- check vendor SAI version
Fall back to warm-reboot instead

meaning: The express reboot script requested syncd pre-shutdown with the express flag (-pxe). syncd will set FAST_API_ENABLE on all ASICs and serialize state to sai-warmboot.bin.
likely_causes: Normal express reboot shutdown sequence
next_steps: Monitor for successful completion: look for checkpoint completion messages
If this hangs, check: docker exec syncd supervisorctl status syncd

meaning: syncd received the APPLY_VIEW notification from orchagent. This triggers NPU bulk programming in express boot mode. The NPU will now be initialized with the restored configuration.
likely_causes: Normal express reboot boot-up -- orchagent finished reconciliation and signaled syncd
next_steps: Verify forwarding is working: show ip route, ping test
Check WARM_RESTART_TABLE state transitions to 'reconciled'

meaning: syncd failed to initialize from sai-warmboot.bin during warm/express boot. The SAI layer could not deserialize the saved ASIC state. The switch will likely fall back to cold boot behavior.
likely_causes: sai-warmboot.bin is corrupt or incomplete (disk I/O error during save)
SDK version mismatch between the image that saved the file and the booting image
File was from a previous failed reboot attempt (stale)
Disk space ran out while writing the file
next_steps: Check if cold boot fallback occurred: cat /proc/cmdline | grep SONIC_BOOT_TYPE
Check file integrity: ls -la /host/warmboot/sai-warmboot.bin
If stale, clean up: sudo rm -f /host/warmboot/sai-warmboot.bin
Verify SDK versions match between images: show version on both images

meaning: During the express boot V1-to-V2 transition window (~1-2s), punt/inject packet headers from the old software version are incompatible with the new NPU expectations. Packets may be dropped or corrupted.
likely_causes: Cross-version express reboot where the punt/inject header format changed between SDK versions
This is a known limitation of express reboot during major version transitions
next_steps: This is usually transient (1-2 seconds during transition)
If persistent, the SDK versions may be too far apart for express reboot
Consider using warm-reboot for major version transitions instead
Check: docker logs syncd 2>&1 | grep -i 'punt\|inject' | tail -20

meaning: orchagent failed to program a route into the ASIC via SAI
likely_causes: ASIC FIB/TCAM table full -- check scale limits
SAI library bug or platform-specific constraint
syncd crashed or not running
Invalid next-hop object ID (neighbor not resolved)
next_steps: Check syncd status: docker exec syncd supervisorctl status syncd
Check ASIC utilization: show system-resources or crm show resources all
Review syncd log: docker exec syncd cat /var/log/syncd.log | tail -50
Check if next-hop neighbor exists: redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL 'NEIGH_TABLE:<interface>:<nexthop_ip>'

meaning: orchagent failed to program a neighbor (ARP/ND) entry into the ASIC
likely_causes: Neighbor table full in ASIC
Invalid interface/router interface object
syncd not responding
next_steps: Check neighbor table capacity: crm show resources all | grep neighbor
Verify ARP entry exists in kernel: ip neigh show <ip>
Check APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL 'NEIGH_TABLE:<intf>:<ip>'

meaning: orchagent is falling behind processing APPL_DB notifications
likely_causes: CPU congestion -- too many routes being programmed simultaneously
syncd slow to respond (ASIC programming delay)
Memory pressure causing swap
next_steps: Check CPU: show processes cpu
Check memory: show system-memory
Check if bulk route update in progress (e.g., BGP convergence)

meaning: Port operational status changed -- link up or down event
likely_causes: Physical link change (cable, transceiver, peer port)
Admin shutdown/no-shutdown
Auto-negotiation failure
next_steps: Check interface status: show interfaces status
Check transceiver: show interfaces transceiver eeprom <interface>
Check for link flapping: show logging | grep <interface>

meaning: orchagent warm restart state change -- reconciliation in progress or complete
likely_causes: Warm restart was initiated via config warm_restart enable
swss container restarted with warm_restart enabled
next_steps: Check warm restart state: show warm_restart state
Wait for reconciliation to complete before making config changes

meaning: Critical Resource Monitoring detected resource usage above threshold
likely_causes: ASIC table approaching capacity (routes, neighbors, ACLs, next-hops)
Resource leak in orchagent
next_steps: Check all CRM resources: crm show resources all
Identify which resource is near capacity
Review if routes/neighbors can be reduced or TCAM profile adjusted

meaning: syncd failed to initialize the SAI API -- cannot communicate with ASIC
likely_causes: Vendor SAI library (libsai.so) missing or corrupt
ASIC hardware failure or not detected
Kernel driver for ASIC not loaded
Incompatible SAI library version
next_steps: Check if ASIC kernel module is loaded: lsmod | grep <vendor>
Check dmesg for hardware errors: dmesg | tail -50
Verify libsai.so exists: docker exec syncd ls -la /usr/lib/libsai*
Try cold reboot: sudo reboot

meaning: SAI API call returned an error status from the vendor ASIC SDK
likely_causes: SAI_STATUS_TABLE_FULL: ASIC table capacity reached
SAI_STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED: Feature not supported on this platform
SAI_STATUS_FAILURE: Generic vendor SDK error
SAI_STATUS_INVALID_PARAMETER: Bad parameter from orchagent
next_steps: Decode the specific SAI status code from the log
Check ASIC resource usage: crm show resources all
Check platform capabilities with vendor documentation
If TABLE_FULL: reduce route/ACL/neighbor count or change TCAM profile

meaning: ASIC hardware notification received -- FDB learning event, port state change, or BFD session event
likely_causes: Normal ASIC operation -- hardware events propagated to software
FDB: MAC address learned/aged/moved
Port: Physical link state change detected by PHY
next_steps: Usually informational -- no action needed unless excessive
High rate of FDB notifications may indicate MAC flapping
High rate of port notifications may indicate link instability

meaning: bgpcfgd failed to render a Jinja2 template for FRR configuration
likely_causes: Invalid or missing fields in CONFIG_DB BGP tables
Custom template modification broke Jinja2 syntax
bgpcfgd code bug after SONiC upgrade
next_steps: Check CONFIG_DB BGP entries: redis-cli -n 4 KEYS 'BGP_*'
Check template: cat /usr/share/sonic/templates/bgpd.conf.j2
Check bgpcfgd logs: journalctl -u bgp | grep bgpcfgd | tail -30
Try regenerating: sonic-cfggen -d -t /usr/share/sonic/templates/bgpd.conf.j2

meaning: bgpcfgd's vtysh command to configure FRR was rejected
likely_causes: FRR (bgpd) rejected the configuration command
Syntax error in generated vtysh command
FRR is not running or crashed
next_steps: Check FRR status: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status
Try the vtysh command manually: docker exec bgp vtysh -c '<command>'
Check FRR log: docker exec bgp cat /var/log/frr/bgpd.log | tail -30

meaning: bgpcfgd detected a CONFIG_DB change and is syncing to FRR
likely_causes: Normal operation -- user made a config change via CLI/API
next_steps: Usually informational -- verify the change took effect in FRR: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show running-config'

meaning: fpmsyncd successfully established a TCP socket connection to zebra's FPM server; route programming pipeline between FRR and APPL_DB is now active
likely_causes: Normal startup sequence after bgp container starts
zebra restarted and fpmsyncd reconnected
Warm restart reconciliation beginning
next_steps: Verify FPM connection is stable: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd
Confirm zebra is running: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status zebra
Check route table is populating: show ip route summary
Verify APPL_DB is receiving routes: redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l

meaning: fpmsyncd lost its TCP connection to zebra's FPM server; new routes from FRR will not reach APPL_DB or the ASIC until reconnection
likely_causes: zebra process crashed or was restarted
bgp container OOM kill or supervisord restart
FPM port 2620 blocked by iptables or namespace issue
fpmsyncd itself hit an exception and tore down the socket
next_steps: Check zebra status: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status zebra
Check fpmsyncd status: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd
Look for zebra crash in logs: docker exec bgp cat /var/log/frr/frr.log | grep -i 'crash\|assert\|abort' | tail -20
Check FPM port is listening: docker exec bgp ss -tnlp | grep 2620
Verify routes are stale by comparing kernel to APPL_DB: ip route show | wc -l vs redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l
Restart fpmsyncd if zebra is healthy: docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart fpmsyncd

meaning: fpmsyncd is writing a route entry to ROUTE_TABLE in APPL_DB; this is the normal path for FRR-learned routes entering the SONiC pipeline
likely_causes: BGP session received a new prefix
Static route configured and activated
Warm restart reconciliation replaying routes
next_steps: Confirm route reached APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL 'ROUTE_TABLE:<prefix>'
Check orchagent picked up the route: redis-cli -n 1 HGETALL 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_ROUTE_ENTRY:{"dest":"<prefix>"}'
Verify end-to-end: show ip route <prefix>

meaning: fpmsyncd is removing a route entry from ROUTE_TABLE in APPL_DB; orchagent will subsequently withdraw the route from the ASIC
likely_causes: BGP withdrew the prefix (peer down, policy change, MED change)
Static route removed from config
Interface carrying the next-hop went down
next_steps: Confirm route was removed from APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 EXISTS 'ROUTE_TABLE:<prefix>'
Check if the BGP session is still up: show bgp summary
Check if next-hop interface is up: show interfaces status
Verify ASIC removal: show ip route <prefix> (should be absent or via different path)

meaning: fpmsyncd could not write a route to APPL_DB due to a Redis error; routes are silently dropped and will not reach orchagent or the ASIC
likely_causes: redis-server process crashed or restarted mid-write
Redis memory limit hit (maxmemory policy evicting keys)
Unix socket /var/run/redis/redis.sock not accessible from bgp container
Redis AUTH mismatch after container restart
next_steps: Check redis-server health: docker exec database redis-cli ping
Check Redis memory: docker exec database redis-cli info memory | grep used_memory_human
Check Redis error log: docker exec database cat /var/log/redis/redis-server.log | tail -30
Verify socket is accessible from bgp container: docker exec bgp ls -la /var/run/redis/redis.sock
Check if routes are missing from APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l
Restart fpmsyncd to force route replay from zebra: docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart fpmsyncd

pattern: Failed to parse.*FPM|fpm.*malformed|invalid FPM message|FPM msg.*length
meaning: fpmsyncd received a FPM netlink message from zebra that it could not parse; the route update is discarded and will not reach APPL_DB
likely_causes: FRR version mismatch between zebra and fpmsyncd -- netlink message format incompatibility
Memory corruption in zebra causing garbled FPM output
Partial write to FPM socket (kernel buffer issue)
Custom FRR patch introducing new message fields fpmsyncd does not understand
next_steps: Check FRR version consistency: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show version'
Check fpmsyncd binary version: docker exec bgp fpmsyncd --version 2>&1
Look for zebra assertion failures near the timestamp: docker exec bgp cat /var/log/frr/frr.log | grep -i 'assert\|FPM' | tail -30
Enable FPM debug in zebra: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'debug zebra fpm'
Check if specific prefixes trigger parse errors by correlating BGP updates: show bgp ipv4 unicast
Restart both zebra and fpmsyncd to reset the FPM socket: docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart zebra fpmsyncd

meaning: fpmsyncd is performing post-restart route reconciliation -- replaying all routes from zebra to APPL_DB to ensure consistency after a warm restart
likely_causes: Warm restart enabled and bgp container restarted
fpmsyncd restarted while zebra remained up (partial restart scenario)
SONiC warm reboot sequence initiated
next_steps: Monitor reconciliation progress: show warm_restart state
Watch APPL_DB route count stabilize: watch -n 2 "redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l"
Do not make route policy changes until reconciliation completes
If reconciliation hangs: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd
Check reconciliation timer has not expired: journalctl -u bgp -n 50 | grep reconcil

meaning: fpmsyncd received a route from zebra with a next-hop that cannot be resolved to a valid interface or neighbor; the route is dropped and will not be programmed into the ASIC
likely_causes: Recursive next-hop chain too deep or contains a loop
Next-hop IP is not reachable via any directly connected interface
Interface carrying the next-hop went down before zebra withdrew the route
ECMP next-hop group partially invalid (some members down)
VRF misconfiguration causing next-hop lookup in wrong table
next_steps: Identify the problematic prefix from logs and inspect next-hop: show ip route <prefix>
Check if next-hop is reachable: ping <nexthop-ip> from management or control plane
Verify neighbor resolution: show arp | grep <nexthop-ip>
Check zebra's view of the next-hop: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip nexthop'
Check if the upstream BGP peer is advertising reachable next-hops: show bgp neighbors <peer> advertised-routes
For VRF routes: verify next-hop VRF is correct with show ip route vrf <vrf-name>
